title: საქართველოს ზოგადი ადმინისტრაციული კოდექსი — თავი III: საჯარო ინფორმაცია
title_en: General Administrative Code of Georgia — Chapter III: Freedom of Information
description: Chapter III of the General Administrative Code of Georgia establishes the right of access to public information held by public bodies. This framework, considered one of the most progressive freedom of information laws in the post-Soviet region at the time of its adoption, provides for a presumption of disclosure, a narrow set of exceptions, and a short response deadline. The framework aligns with the EU Open Data Directive (Directive (EU) 2019/1024) and Council of Europe standards as part of Georgia's EU approximation commitments.

provisions.title: Article 37. Right to Public Information
provisions.content: Everyone has the right to receive public information without providing a reason. The right to receive public information may be restricted only in the cases and manner prescribed by this Code and other laws, where the restriction is necessary in a democratic society for legitimate aims including national security, public safety, the prevention of crime, the protection of privacy, or the protection of confidential business information. Public information is considered to be official documents held by a public institution, information received, processed, created or dispatched by a public institution, whether in paper or electronic form. Public information is open by default, and any restriction of access must be justified on specific legal grounds. The right to access public information shall be exercised free of charge, except for charges for reproduction.

provisions.title: Article 38. Proactive Disclosure
provisions.content: Public institutions shall proactively disclose, without waiting for a specific request, the following categories of information: (a) the institution's legal acts and legal basis for operation; (b) the institution's organizational structure and contact information; (c) information on vacancies and selection procedures; (d) budget information and audit reports; (e) annual reports on the institution's activities; (f) information on public procurement; (g) information on grants and subsidies provided; (h) information on meetings of collegial bodies; and (i) any other information designated by law as subject to mandatory proactive disclosure. Public institutions shall publish this information on their official websites in an accessible, structured, and machine-readable format where possible. Public institutions shall update proactively disclosed information within ten working days of any change.

provisions.title: Article 39. Submission and Processing of Requests
provisions.content: A request for public information may be submitted orally, in writing, or electronically to any public institution. Written and electronic requests shall be responded to within ten working days from the date of receipt. Where a request cannot be fulfilled within ten working days, the public institution shall notify the requestor within five working days of receipt of the request, explaining the reason for the delay and indicating when the information will be provided. A request may be refused only on the grounds specified in this Code. A refusal to provide information shall be in writing, shall cite the specific legal provision justifying the refusal, and shall indicate the person responsible for the decision and the procedure for appealing the decision. If the requested information is held by another public institution, the request shall be transferred to that institution and the requestor notified within five working days.

provisions.title: Article 40. Exceptions to Access
provisions.content: Access to public information may be restricted where the information: (a) is classified as state secret under the Law of Georgia on State Secrets; (b) constitutes personal data and its disclosure would violate the right to privacy protected by the Law on Personal Data Protection; (c) constitutes commercial secrets or confidential business information; (d) relates to ongoing law enforcement investigations where disclosure would prejudice those investigations; (e) relates to pre-decisional deliberations of public bodies where disclosure would significantly harm the decision-making process; or (f) is otherwise restricted from disclosure by specific legislation. Exceptions to access shall be interpreted narrowly and shall be applied only where the harm from disclosure outweighs the public interest in transparency. Public bodies shall disclose any portions of a document that do not fall within an exception, separating them from portions that are restricted.

provisions.title: Article 41. Appeal of Decisions
provisions.content: A person whose request for information has been refused, partially refused, or not responded to within the prescribed period may: (a) appeal the decision to a superior administrative body within 10 working days; (b) file an administrative complaint with the Public Defender (Ombudsman) of Georgia; or (c) apply to the court for review of the decision within one month. Courts shall treat appeals relating to access to information as a matter of priority and shall examine them within 10 working days. Where a court finds that access was unjustifiably refused, it shall order the public body to provide the requested information within three working days. Requestors who prevail on appeal may recover reasonable legal costs from the public institution. The Public Defender shall prepare an annual report on the state of freedom of information, which shall be submitted to the Parliament.

provisions.title: Article 42. Open Data
provisions.content: Public institutions shall publish information subject to proactive disclosure in open, machine-readable, and reusable formats on the national open data portal (data.gov.ge), in accordance with the Open Data Policy of Georgia. Documents and data published as open data may be freely used, reused and redistributed by anyone, subject only to attribution requirements. High-value datasets, as designated by the government, shall be published as open data with high priority. The Government of Georgia shall designate categories of high-value datasets based on their economic, social, and environmental value and the demand from re-users. This article implements Georgia's obligations under the EU-Georgia Association Agreement to align with the EU Open Data Directive (Directive (EU) 2019/1024). Reference: Directive (EU) 2019/1024 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 20 June 2019 on open data and the re-use of public sector information.

provisions.title: Article 43. Fees for Information
provisions.content: Access to public information shall be free of charge. Public institutions may charge only for the actual cost of reproducing or transmitting information in a physical format, such as photocopying or printing. Fees for reproduction and transmission shall not exceed the actual cost to the public institution and shall not be set at a level that effectively discourages requests for information. Fees shall not be charged for: (a) information provided electronically in digital format; (b) information provided for the purpose of journalistic investigation or academic research; (c) information requested by individuals with low income or by civil society organizations for non-commercial purposes; or (d) information of direct personal relevance to the requestor. The government shall set by regulation the maximum fees that public institutions may charge for reproduction and transmission of information.

provisions.title: Article 44. Register of Documents
provisions.content: Each public institution shall maintain and publish a register of the categories of documents it holds, enabling requestors to identify what information is available and how to request it. The register shall be published on the institution's website and shall be updated at least annually. The register shall include: (a) a description of the categories of documents held by the institution; (b) the format in which each category of documents is held; (c) the procedure for requesting access to each category of documents; and (d) whether each category of documents is subject to proactive disclosure, restricted access, or must be requested individually. The register shall be freely accessible without a need to submit a request. Public institutions shall cooperate with the Public Defender in maintaining accurate registers of the documents they hold.

provisions.title: Article 45. Electronic Access to Information
provisions.content: Public institutions shall provide access to public information by electronic means and shall make all proactively disclosed information available on their websites. Public institutions shall accept requests for information submitted electronically and shall, where technically feasible, provide responses in electronic format. The government shall establish a unified online portal for submitting requests for public information to all public institutions, enabling citizens to track the status of their requests and to receive responses electronically. All public institutions shall ensure that their websites are accessible to persons with disabilities in accordance with international accessibility standards. Information published electronically shall be structured and tagged in a manner that facilitates search and retrieval.

provisions.title: Article 46. Liability for Violations
provisions.content: A public official who, without lawful justification, refuses to provide public information, provides false information, or delays the provision of information beyond the prescribed period shall be subject to disciplinary liability and may be liable for a fine. A public official who destroys, damages, or alters public information to prevent its disclosure shall be subject to criminal liability under the Criminal Code of Georgia. The Public Defender may investigate violations of freedom of information rights and may recommend remedial measures and disciplinary action to the relevant public institution. Systematic failures by public institutions to fulfil their obligations under this chapter shall be reported to the Parliament of Georgia. Public institutions that fail to comply with court orders relating to access to information shall be held in contempt of court.

provisions.title: Article 47. Annual Reporting
provisions.content: Each public institution shall submit an annual report to the Parliament of Georgia and to the Public Defender on its activities relating to freedom of information, including: (a) the number of requests received, and responses provided; (b) the number of requests refused, with reasons; (c) the number of appeals received and outcomes; (d) measures taken to improve access to information; and (e) the status of proactive disclosure obligations. The Civil Service Bureau shall compile a consolidated annual report on freedom of information across all public institutions and shall publish recommendations for improvement. The consolidated report shall be submitted to the Parliament and published on the government's official website. Georgia shall cooperate with international organizations monitoring freedom of information as part of its international transparency commitments.

definitions.definition: Official documents held by a public institution, information received, processed, created or dispatched by a public institution, whether in paper or electronic form.
Information published by public institutions in open, machine-readable and reusable formats that may be freely used, reused and redistributed by anyone, subject only to attribution requirements.
